--- SOURCE 1: Veterinary telerehabilitation was as satisfactory as in- ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/

SUMMARY:
To evaluate client satisfaction with telerehabilitation consultations compared to in-person consultations for veterinary rehabilitation referrals. This study demonstrated high client satisfaction with using telemedicine for canine rehabilitation consultations that was comparable to that for in-person consultations. To the authors’ knowledge, this is the first study that evaluated client satisfaction with veterinary telerehabilitation consultations. We hypothesized that client satisfaction with telerehabilitation consultations is comparable to that for in-person rehabilitation evaluations. For the question of overall satisfaction, “My treatment goals were achieved, and I would recommend this service to others,” the median satisfaction score for in-person consultation was 9.5 (range: 7 to 10), compared to a median score for telerehabilitation evaluation of 10.0 (range: 8 to 10) (_P_ = 0.32).

Veterinary telerehabilitation was as satisfactory as in-person consultations - PMC

Veterinary telerehabilitation was as satisfactory as in-person consultations
============================================================================

[Samantha Y Wong](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Wong%20SY%22%5BAuthor%5D)

### Samantha Y Wong

1 Integrative and Rehabilitative Medicine Department, The Schwarzman Animal Medical Center, 510 East 62nd Street, New York, New York 10002, USA

Find articles by [Samantha Y Wong](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Wong%20SY%22%5BAuthor%5D)

1,✉, [Leilani X Alvarez](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Alvarez%20LX%22%5BAuthor%5D)

### Leilani X Alvarez

1 Integrative and Rehabilitative Medicine Department, The Schwarzman Animal Medical Center, 510 East 62nd Street, New York, New York 10002, USA

Find articles by [Leilani X Alvarez](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Alvarez%20LX%22%5BAuthor%5D)

1

*   Author information
*   Copyright and License information

1 Integrative and Rehabilitative Medicine Department, The Schwarzman Animal Medical Center, 510 East 62nd Street, New York, New York 10002, USA

✉
Address all correspondence to Dr. Samantha Wong; email: Samantha.Wong@amcny.org

✉
Corresponding author.

Copyright and/or publishing rights held by the Canadian Veterinary Medical Association

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)

PMCID: PMC10286140 PMID: [37397700](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37397700/)

Abstract
--------

### Objective

To evaluate client satisfaction with telerehabilitation consultations compared to in-person consultations for veterinary rehabilitation referrals.

### Animals

We surveyed the owners of 32 client-owned dogs.

### Procedure

Dog owners were scheduled for telemedicine (telerehabilitation group) or in-person (control group) based on a combination of owner requests and medical recommendations. Medical records were obtained before evaluation. Owners were sent an electronic questionnaire following in-person or telerehabilitation consultations. A total of 32 surveys were received (16 for each group). The response rate was 55% (32/58 surveys sent). Mann-Whitney U tests were used to compare ordinal characteristics between satisfied and unsatisfied clients. Descriptive statistics for the client population, including ranges and medians, were calculated for owner travel distance and patient signalment.

### Results

Satisfaction regarding scheduling appointments was higher in the telerehabilitation group compared to the group receiving in-person consultations (_P_< 0.001). For all other aspects of client satisfaction, there were no significant differences between groups.

### Conclusion

This study demonstrated high client satisfaction with using telemedicine for canine rehabilitation consultations that was comparable to that for in-person consultations.

### Clinical relevance

Telerehabilitation is a viable option that can be easily implemented by rehabilitation practitioners for assessment, progression, and monitoring of canine patients. Further studies are indicated to evaluate the efficacy of telerehabilitation.

Résumé
------

La téléréadaptation vétérinaire a été aussi satisfaisante que les consultations en personne

### Objectif

Évaluer la satisfaction des clients à l’égard des consultations de téléréadaptation par rapport aux consultations en personne pour les patients aiguillés en réadaptation vétérinaire.

### Animaux

Enquêtes auprès des propriétaires de 32 chiens appartenant à des clients.

### Procédure

Les propriétaires étaient programmés en télémédecine (groupe de téléréadaptation) ou en personne (groupe témoin) en fonction d’une combinaison de demandes de propriétaires ou de recommandations médicales. Les dossiers médicaux ont été obtenus avant l’évaluation. Les propriétaires ont reçu un questionnaire électronique à la suite de consultations en personne ou par téléréadaptation. Au total, 32 sondages ont été reçus (16 pour chaque groupe). Le taux de réponse a été de 55 % (32 sondages sur 58). Les tests Mann-Whitney U ont été utilisés pour comparer les caractéristiques ordinales entre les patients satisfaits et ceux qui ne l’étaient pas. Des statistiques descriptives pour la population de clients, y compris la portée et les médianes, ont été calculées pour la distance de déplacement du propriétaire et la signalisation du patient.

### Résultats

La satisfaction à l’égard des rendez-vous était plus élevée dans le groupe de la téléréadaptation comparativement aux consultations en personne (_P_< 0,001). Pour tous les autres aspects de la satisfaction des clients, il n’y avait pas de différences significatives entre les groupes.

### Conclusion

Cette étude a démontré une satisfaction élevée des clients qui utilisent la télémédecine pour des consultations de réadaptation canine, comparable aux consultations en personne.

### Pertinence clinique

La téléréadaptation est une option viable qui peut être facilement mise en œuvre par les praticiens de la réadaptation pour l’évaluation, la progression et le suivi des patients canins. D’autres études sont indiquées pour évaluer l’efficacité de la téléréadaptation.

(Traduit par les auteurs)

Introduction
------------

Telemedicine using 2-way video conferencing can extend healthcare services to patients and healthcare providers who otherwise might not have access to these services. The core purpose of telemedicine is to improve the health of individuals and communities by providing education and treatment recommendations ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b1-cvj_07_654)). With increased use of telehealth during the COVID-19 global pandemic, patient satisfaction with traditional healthcare is a growing concern ([2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b2-cvj_07_654)). In small animal veterinary practices alone, telehealth services increased from 12% before the pandemic to 38% between March 15 and June 15, 2020, among both general practice and specialty hospitals ([3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b3-cvj_07_654)). Veterinarians must continue to embrace telehealth services to stay relevant for the needs of clients and their animals.

Telemedicine has been used in veterinary specialties such as radiology, cardiology, and pathology for several decades ([4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b4-cvj_07_654)). Expanding the use of telemedicine beyond diagnostics to treatments and consultations is a logical evolution. A previous study demonstrated that owners who used telemedicine for postsurgical recheck appointments were satisfied with the virtual appointments and felt their dogs were less fearful than at in-clinic appointments ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b5-cvj_07_654)). This same study, which evaluated 30 client-owned dogs, demonstrated overall high client satisfaction, with clients noting improved communication and feeling better-informed regarding their pets’ illnesses ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b5-cvj_07_654)). Telemedicine has also been used for behavioral treatment of aggression, with comparable efficacy noted between in-person and remote service groups ([6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b6-cvj_07_654)). In addition, telemedicine may provide benefits to veterinary businesses by being more cost-effective and concurrently increasing access for clients, with associated improvements in consistency of follow-up care and owner compliance ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b5-cvj_07_654),[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b7-cvj_07_654),[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b8-cvj_07_654)).

As with all aspects of healthcare during the COVID-19 pandemic, veterinarians have been faced with surging demands for care. In 2020 alone, veterinary medical appointments increased by 4.5% and wait times increased 20 to 45% beyond regular times, causing a backlog of appointments for non-urgent issues such as arthritis, weight concerns, nutrition counseling, and advice on medication regimes ([9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b9-cvj_07_654)). Veterinary telemedicine can meet the needs of clients for these non-urgent types of appointments. Veterinary rehabilitation primarily consists of non-urgent referrals and is well-suited for telemedicine consultation.

In multiple studies in humans, telerehabilitation was effective for musculoskeletal, neurological, and cardiovascular rehabilitation ([10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b10-cvj_07_654)–[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b12-cvj_07_654)). Client satisfaction with veterinary telemedicine has not been previously studied; however, based on human studies, is expected to yield substantial benefits. To the authors’ knowledge, this is the first study that evaluated client satisfaction with veterinary telerehabilitation consultations. We hypothesized that client satisfaction with telerehabilitation consultations is comparable to that for in-person rehabilitation evaluations.

Materials and methods
---------------------

### Sample

All dogs scheduled for rehabilitation consultation at the Schwarzman Animal Medical Center (AMC) in New York, New York, USA, between September 2021 and February 2022, were eligible for the study. All telerehabilitation patients had been physically evaluated by a New York State veterinarian within the previous 4 mo. All owners consented to receive a satisfaction survey and have their anonymous responses used and published for research purposes.

### Study design

This was a prospective client satisfaction survey approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the AMC (PROTOCOL AMC_10_28_20). All dogs were evaluated by either a Diplomate of the American College of Veterinary Sports Medicine and Rehabilitation (DACVSMR) or a DACVSMR resident. The consultations were either in-person or _via_ Televet (Televet, Austin, Texas, USA) telemedicine practice management software with 2-way video conferencing. Owners were assigned telerehabilitation or in-person consultations based on a combination of requests by the owners or recommendations from the referring veterinarians. These appointments were booked by 1 client care coordinator. A veterinary patient client relationship (VPCR) was established within the confines of New York State regulation as outlined by the American Veterinary Medical Association ([13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b13-cvj_07_654)). A modified version of the Hospital for Special Surgery’s (New York, New York, USA) Q-Review Survey (Quality Reviews, New York, New York, USA), previously used to evaluate patient satisfaction with telerehabilitation, was used in this study ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b14-cvj_07_654)). All owners were instructed to answer a 9-question survey through the Televet platform (see Appendix 1, available online from: [www.canadianveterinarians.net](http://www.canadianveterinarians.net/)).

Information regarding concerns, goals, and previous medical conditions was obtained from electronic medical records before telerehabilitation evaluation. The dogs were being managed for rehabilitation disorders including palliative pain management, geriatric care, obesity management, and orthopedic and neurologic conditions. All telemedicine consultations were 30 min in length, and in-person owner conversations were also 30 min (+/– 5 min) in length. The in-person conversation followed an allotted 30-minute examination of the dog, without the owner present, for a total consultation time of 1 h. This followed the standard recheck format for the department. Pricing for telemedicine consultations and in-person consultations was comparable.

### Data collection

Upon completion of a consultation, an electronic survey was sent to the dog owners. Surveys were closed-ended and sent from September 2021 to February 2022. The same survey was sent to owners in the in-person and the telemedicine groups. All telerehabilitation and in-person consultations were limited to recheck consultations to ensure establishment of a VPCR. The questionnaire was sent to owners within 1 h after their consultations, as a link _via_ text message or email with an explanation of its use. Travel distance for each client was calculated using the addresses provided in the medical record.

Items on the questionnaire were scored on a 5-point Likert scale, indicating 5 for “most satisfied” and 1 for “least satisfied.” One question was graded on a 10-point scale, as per the Hospital for Special Surgery questionnaire, as a measure of overall satisfaction ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b14-cvj_07_654)). Clients that did not fully complete the survey or completed both telerehabilitation and in-person surveys were excluded.

### Statistical analyses

Descriptive statistics for the client population, including ranges, average, and medians, were calculated for owner travel distance and patient signalments. A power calculation sample size was determined based on an effect of 30%, with α = 0.10, as per a previous study evaluating telemedicine recheck satisfaction ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b5-cvj_07_654),[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b15-cvj_07_654)). Mann-Whitney U tests were used to compare ordinal characteristics between satisfied and unsatisfied clients. All tests were 2-tailed and statistical significance was set to α = 0.05.

Results
-------

### Owner responses

Of the 58 surveys sent, 32 surveys were returned (55% response rate). All 32 owners of canine patients completed the survey within 1 wk after consultation. A total of 16 surveys were received for each group.

The age of dogs ranged from 1 to 17 y, with a median age of 9 y. The presenting complaint for each consultation ranged from mobility disorders secondary to multifocal osteoarthritis (8/32), myelopathies (7/32), and other musculoskeletal conditions (12/32), to palliative nursing care that included long-term home management with diets and medications (5/32). Twelve of the 16 dogs receiving telerehabilitation consultations were previously evaluated by an AMC rehabilitation veterinarian, and the other 4 were referred by either primary veterinarians or specialists. All 16 of the dogs receiving in-person consultations were previously evaluated by an AMC rehabilitation veterinarian. Two of the telerehabilitation patients and 1 in-person patient had an open diagnosis. In-person client travel distance ranged from 0.1 to 33.5 km (median: 11.5 km). Telerehabilitation client travel distance ranged from 0.8 to 122.8 km (median: 12.1 km). The average wait time to schedule a telemedicine appointment was 2 to 3 wk, compared to 4 to 6 wk for an in-person appointment.

### Owner satisfaction

Of the 9 questions on the questionnaire, the score for only 1 question, related to scheduling, differed between in-person and telerehabilitation evaluation (_P_< 0.001). Regarding ease of scheduling, the median satisfaction score for in-person consultation was 4.0 (range: 3 to 5, 95% CI: 3.16 to 4.16), compared to a median score for telerehabilitation of 5.0 (range: 5 to 5, 95% CI: 3.317 to 4.023) ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#t1-cvj_07_654)). For the question of overall satisfaction, “My treatment goals were achieved, and I would recommend this service to others,” the median satisfaction score for in-person consultation was 9.5 (range: 7 to 10), compared to a median score for telerehabilitation evaluation of 10.0 (range: 8 to 10) (_P_ = 0.32). For all other questions, there were no significant differences between in-person and telerehabilitation evaluation groups ([Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#t1-cvj_07_654)).

#### Table 1.

Analysis of patient satisfaction in response to each survey question by group.

| Survey question | Group | Mean score | Median score | _P_-value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1. How would you rate your ability to schedule an appointment? | In-person | 3.62 | 4 | < 0.001 |
| Telerehabilitation | 5 | 5 |
| 2. How would you rate the amount of time your rehabilitation doctor spent with you and your pet? | In-person | 4.75 | 5 | 0.734 |
| Telerehabilitation | 4.875 | 5 |
| 3. How well did your doctor explain the findings of your pet’s initial evaluation? | In-person | 4.75 | 5 | 0.763 |
| Telerehabilitation | 4.81 | 5 |
| 4. How would you rate our concern for your questions and worries? | In-person | 4.81 | 5 | 0.342 |
| Telerehabilitation | 5 | 5 |
| 5. Overall, how would you rate your experience during this visit? | In-person | 4.81 | 5 | 0.806 |
| Telerehabilitation | 4.81 | 5 |
| 6. How would you rate the courtesy and friendliness of the doctor? | In-person | 4.87 | 5 | 0.763 |
| Telerehabilitation | 4.93 | 5 |  |
| 7. How would you rate your confidence in the skill of your doctor? | In-person | 4.87 | 5 | 0.546 |
| Telerehabilitation | 5 | 5 |
| 8. How would you rate the degree to which our outpatient therapy services have helped you and your pet progress towards your goals? | In-person | 4.56 | 5 | 0.534 |
| Telerehabilitation | 4.75 | 5 |
| 9. “My treatment goals were achieved, and I would recommend this service to others with similar issues.” How much would you agree with this statement? | In-person | 9.25 | 10 | 0.327 |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/table/t1-cvj_07_654/)

Discussion
----------

This study demonstrated a level of client satisfaction with telemedicine that was comparable to that for in-person rehabilitation evaluations, thereby supporting our hypothesis. Furthermore, clients in the telerehabilitation group had higher satisfaction with ease of scheduling appointments. All other survey questions had comparable satisfaction between in-person and telemedicine groups. These results agreed with those from an evaluation of patient satisfaction with human telerehabilitation ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b14-cvj_07_654)).

In this study, a telemedicine consultation did not involve any staff members aside from the veterinarian and did not cost the dog owner or hospital any additional expenses. A previous study noted that dog owners were willing to pay an additional $38.04 for veterinary telemedicine consultation, demonstrating the financial viability of telemedicine ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b7-cvj_07_654)). In an urban setting such as the facility in which this study was conducted, the cost and time associated with transportation can be crucial factors for owners traveling for in-person consultation. In a previous study ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b5-cvj_07_654)), the average travel time saved with telemedicine was ~50 min; we suspect a similar benefit occurred in this study given the limited availability of rehabilitation specialists in urban settings. In this study, the longest travel distance for telerehabilitation was 122.8 km, which was ~4 × the distance traveled for in-person evaluations (33.5 km). In addition, the wait time for a telemedicine consultation was half (2 to 3 wk) that for an in-person evaluation (4 to 6 wk). Convenience regarding time required for travel (for finding parking, public transportation, or ride shares) may have influenced scheduling satisfaction, although transportation satisfaction was not evaluated specifically.

The efficacy of owner-directed rehabilitation has not yet been evaluated, but the approach is commonly used by rehabilitation practitioners in home exercise plans. In the study from Donham and Wickett ([16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b16-cvj_07_654)), animal owners were directed to perform a series of behavior modifications at home using positive reinforcement, daily exercise, and dietary modifications. During telerehabilitation consultations, owners were frequently guided to help facilitate evaluation of functional mobility and visual assessment of discomfort over video. Owner involvement and the ability to assess their pet’s progress in the home environment may have added to their overall experience and satisfaction with the service. Bennet _et al_ ([17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b17-cvj_07_654)) previously evaluated owner satisfaction in reference to dog training facilities. In that study, owner satisfaction was multifactorial, and there was a correlation between satisfaction in the facility and satisfaction with the progress made by the handler and the dog. In another study ([18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b18-cvj_07_654)), satisfaction with dog ownership was unrelated to perceived emotional closeness, shared activities, and class attendance ([18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b18-cvj_07_654)), but there was a strong correlation with high perceived costs of ownership ([18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b18-cvj_07_654)). Further research evaluating the efficacy of telerehabilitation and owner satisfaction should be considered.

Dogs in this study were all evaluated at recheck consultations and thereby had established VPCRs. In North America, the requirement for a physical examination by the veterinarian currently varies among states and provinces. In New York State, an in-person evaluation is not required ([13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b13-cvj_07_654)). At the time of the last Canadian Veterinary Medical Association position statement in 2021, Ontario, British Columbia, and Alberta allowed temporary establishment of a virtual VPCR ([19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10286140/#b19-cvj_07_654)). Further research on the satisfaction with and success of telemedicine consultations could provide evidence regarding the utility of establishing virtual VPCRs.

There were several limitations in this study. Most notably, the small sample size is associated with the risk of Type II error (false negative) in the data analysis and the potential of not accurately representing the general population. In addition, the study was not randomized, presenting a risk of selection bias. Another shortcoming was the fact that 4 of the 32 dogs were not previously evaluated in person by the veterinarians performing the telerehabilitation consultations, which may bring into question the risks of reliance on referral evaluation and history. Notwithstanding these limitations, the results of the present study supported our hypothesis that client satisfaction with telerehabilitation is high and is comparable to that for in-person evaluation. CVJ

Footnotes
---------

The authors did not receive financial assistance for this study.

Veterinary Telemedicine: How It’s Transforming Pet Care
=======================================================

![Image 5: Veterinarian looking stressed at work, highlighting the importance of veterinary burnout prevention.](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img/https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/u7266122496_realistic_image_of_stressed_veterinarian_-v_6.1_e052042f-b405-41fb-b4ac-a7b42a007239_0.png?ver=1736258502)

Juan Gervasoni

Specialist

January 22, 2025

![Image 6: Pet owner conducting a telemedicine consultation with a veterinarian using a laptop, with a small dog sitting beside her.](https://sp-ao.shortpixel.ai/client/to_webp,q_glossy,ret_img,w_1707,h_2560/https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/medium-shot-girl-bed-with-laptop-dog-scaled.jpg)

Telemedicine has become a transformative force in the veterinary field, offering new ways to deliver care, connect with pet owners, and streamline clinic operations. In an era where convenience and accessibility are paramount, telemedicine bridges the gap between traditional veterinary services and modern technology. This blog explores how veterinary telemedicine works, its benefits, and how veterinary clinics can implement this technology effectively.

### **The Growing Importance of Telemedicine in Veterinary Medicine**

Veterinary telemedicine involves using technology to provide remote consultation, diagnosis, and follow-up care for animals. This approach has gained significant traction in recent years, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic, which accelerated virtual services across the medical field. Telemedicine enables veterinary professionals to:

*   **Enhance Accessibility**: Provide care to pet owners in rural or underserved areas.
*   **Improve Efficiency**: Reduce clinic overcrowding by addressing non-emergency cases remotely.
*   **Boost Client Engagement**: Foster stronger relationships with pet owners through regular, convenient communication.
*   **Support Continuity of Care**: Allow for seamless follow-ups without requiring in-person visits.

### **Key Benefits of Telemedicine for Veterinary Professionals**

*   **Increased Flexibility******Relief vets, in particular, can leverage telemedicine to expand their services. Telemedicine allows vets to provide consultations from virtually anywhere, aligning with their lifestyle.
*   **Improved Work-Life Balance******Telemedicine improves work-life balance by reducing commute times and offering more manageable schedules. This aligns with insights from [_Veterinary Work-Life Balance Tips: Manage Stress & Tim_](https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/veterinary-work-life-balance-tips/)e, which emphasizes balancing professional and personal responsibilities.
*   **Streamlined Workflow******Integrating telemedicine tools can help clinics optimize their workflows. For tips on improving clinic efficiency, refer to [_Relief Veterinarian First Day Tips: Starting Your New Job Right_](https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/relief-veterinarian-first-day-tips/)_._
*   **Enhanced Client Communication******Telemedicine offers a convenient platform for discussing minor concerns, treatment plans, or follow-ups. For more insights on effective communication, check out [_Pet Parents Effective Communication Tips._](https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/effective-communication-with-pet-parents/)

### **Implementing Telemedicine in Veterinary Clinics**

*   **Choose the Right Platform******Invest in a secure, user-friendly telemedicine platform that complies with veterinary regulations. Choosing the right platform is essential for seamless consultations.
*   **Train Your Team******Ensure all team members are familiar with the technology and understand how to conduct virtual consultations effectively. Foster a culture of growth and adaptability within the team.
*   **Set Clear Guidelines******Define what services can and cannot be offered via telemedicine to avoid misunderstandings with clients.
*   **Promote the Service******Educate your clients about telemedicine options through social media, newsletters, and in-clinic materials. Highlight its convenience and accessibility to encourage adoption.
*   **Incorporate Telemedicine into SOPs******Create standard operating procedures for telemedicine consultations to ensure consistency and efficiency.

### **Challenges and Limitations of Telemedicine**

While telemedicine offers numerous benefits, it’s essential to recognize its limitations. Not all cases can be managed remotely, and hands-on examinations remain critical for accurate diagnoses. Establishing clear protocols for when in-person visits are necessary helps maintain the highest standards of care.

### **The Future of Telemedicine in Veterinary Practice**

As telemedicine continues to evolve, its integration into veterinary practices will likely expand. From wearable tech for pets to AI-assisted diagnostics, the possibilities are vast.

### **Conclusion**

Telemedicine is reshaping the way veterinary care is delivered, offering unparalleled convenience and efficiency for both professionals and pet owners. By embracing this innovative approach, veterinary clinics can enhance their services, improve client satisfaction, and adapt to the demands of modern pet care. To stay ahead in the industry, consider integrating telemedicine into your practice and exploring its full potential.

**For more insights on improving veterinary practices, check out our other blogs:**

*   [_Battling Burnout in the Veterinary Field_](https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/burnout-prevention-veterinarian/)
*   [_Managing Compassion Fatigue: Tips for Veterinary Professionals_](https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/managing-compassion-fatigue-tips-for-veterinary-professionals/)
*   [_Essential Tools and Tech for Relief Veterinarians_](https://www.flexvetstaffing.com/essential-tools-and-tech-for-relief-veterinarians/)

FULL CONTENT:
# Using Telemedicine to Boost Veterinary Team Efficiency

![Using Telemedicine to Boost Veterinary Team Efficiency](/sites/default/files/styles/825_485_focal_point/public/2024-08/Using-Telemedicine-to-Boost-Veterinary-Team-Efficiency-min.jpg?h=c44fcfa1&itok=-heJDmDX)

Specialty teleconsultation has long been available to veterinarians, but client telehealth access only recently emerged as a game-changer after rules and regulations were relaxed during the COVID-19 pandemic. Because laws surrounding the veterinary-client-patient relationship (VCPR) and telemedicine vary by state, some veterinarians may feel wary of the technology. However, for practices willing to navigate the laws, remote consultations can provide clients and pets convenient care access, and boost veterinary team efficiency.

Telemedicine’s many applications for boosting veterinary team efficiency in practice include offering solutions to reduce congestion, improving care access, enhancing care quality, streamlining processes, and optimizing team resources. Here, we explore a few of telemedicine’s benefits for your veterinary practice.

### 1. Reducing schedule congestion

One of the most easily appreciable telemedicine benefits is reducing in-person patient visits. The visit process, including check-in, rooming, physical exam, treatment, and check-out, takes time and physical space. If the reason for the visit could be handled remotely, you can open up time and space for pets who need more involved care. Case types amenable to telehealth consultation include follow-ups, nutrition and weight discussions, health education, dermatology, ophthalmology, minor illness, rehabilitation, palliative care, and behavior issues.

Telehealth consultations require only a few minutes of the veterinarian’s time and minimal support team involvement. This allows the practice to open time slots for sick patients and urgent cases while boosting veterinary team efficiency—and team members can go home on time.

### 2. Expanding care access

Telemedicine allows clients to access veterinary care from their homes, which expands care access for clients facing barriers, such as mobility issues, remote locations, lack of transportation, child-care duties, work, or busy schedules. The option for timely care without travel improves client satisfaction and keeps pets healthier. Also, pets who get needed care are less likely to experience urgent or emergent health concerns that disrupt the clinic schedule and overwhelm the team.

### 3. Streamlining clinic workflows

Telemedicine can help streamline workflow and improve veterinary team efficiency by reducing the time spent on routine cases and freeing support staff to focus on other critical tasks. Additionally, telemedicine has gained such a foothold that developers are taking notice and producing veterinary-specific platforms that meet practice and client needs. Many of these platforms boast integrated scheduling, online billing, and automated record-keeping, further simplifying administrative tasks.

### 4. Improving staff utilization

Telemedicine has made previously unheard-of remote work possible for veterinary professionals. Veterinary practices can optimize their staff by allowing veterinarians and technicians to work remotely during specified days or hours to handle telemedicine consultations and associated tasks, such as prescription authorizations or record-keeping. The team members assigned to the clinic can focus on seeing patients, while those at home can address the administrative burden.

Some hospitals have created entire positions for remote teletriage and other administrative tasks that cloud-based veterinary management software makes possible. The flexibility to work from home can improve team morale and reduce burnout, and lead to a more motivated and efficient veterinary team.

### 5. Expanding practice capabilities

Telemedicine includes client-facing services and vet-to-vet consultations to help with complex or challenging cases. Most veterinary professionals are familiar with teleradiology for X-ray interpretation, but many other specialists also offer remote availability. A specialist consultation early in a case can streamline diagnostics and treatment by providing clear direction, helping to eliminate repeat tests and visits that derail the clinic’s schedule and frustrate clients. With specialist input, the veterinary team can spend less time managing complex cases.

### 6. Offering cost-effective care

Telemedicine is a cost-effective solution for clients and practices. Virtual consultations typically cost less than in-person visits, making veterinary care more broadly accessible for clients. Telemedicine can also reduce the clinic's overhead costs, including expenses associated with additional staffing or extended hours to accommodate a higher patient load. Increased profitability combined with a high standard of care is a recipe for a positive clinic culture and an efficient, driven team.

Telemedicine is a powerful tool that can significantly enhance client satisfaction and veterinary team efficiency. As the veterinary field continues to evolve and to improve care delivery while using fewer human and material resources, embracing telemedicine will ensure long-term practice success.

![Samantha Walker, RVT](/sites/default/files/styles/400_400_focal_point/public/author-images/Sam%20pic.jpeg?h=df3c660d&itok=k4LpoAuw)

Sam joined the IDEXX marketing team after 9 years of working as an RVT in small animal general practices in Ontario, Canada. She has a passion for creating content focused on how software and technology can make life easier for veterinary teams.

![Image 2](https://cdn-kdlcf.nitrocdn.com/cMcYFIddemcOYMBMSLWkeCfvQYhMPlRu/assets/images/optimized/rev-a07a029/gv-wp-landing.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/10173647/post-hero.png)

How Veterinary Telehealth Services and Answering Solutions Are Revolutionizing Client Care?
===========================================================================================

GuardianVets Team Feb 4, 2025

*   [Veterinary Growth](https://guardianvets.com/category/veterinary-growth/)

 The veterinary landscape has seen immense change over the past few years. Practices face increasing demands from pet owners seeking convenient access, after-hours support, and real-time guidance about their pets' health. At the same time, veterinary teams grapple with burnout, compassion fatigue, staffing shortages, and the struggle to deliver exceptional care while managing a growing workload. In this environment, virtual answering and [**veterinary telehealth services**](https://guardianvets.com/) have emerged as game-changing practice solutions—allowing teams to offload administrative tasks, ensure patients get timely care, and keep loyal clients connected 24/7. As pet ownership continues to grow, these technologies are revolutionizing veterinary care and setting a new standard for what clients expect. 
**The Rise of Telehealth in Veterinary Medicine**
-------------------------------------------------

 Telehealth and telemedicine involve the remote diagnosis and treatment of patients using telecommunications technology. In human healthcare, telehealth services have grown exponentially—with the global market expected to reach [**$636 billion by 2028**](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949761223000202). Veterinary telehealth has seen comparable growth, driven by pet owners' demand for convenience and accessible guidance about their pets' health. A [**recent survey**](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11289980/) found 33% of pet owners are interested in telehealth services from their veterinarian. Additional drivers include: 
*   The increase in mobile device usage and internet connectivity is making virtual care possible.
*   Consumer comfort with human telemedicine applications leading to openness toward animal telehealth.
*   Shortages of veterinary staff make offices eager for workflow solutions.

 Today, veterinary practices utilize telehealth for services like video consultations, patient record analysis, chronic disease management, post-op check-ins, and client education. The remote nature makes care more convenient for clients while generating new revenue streams for practices. It also reduces unnecessary appointments to improve in-clinic efficiency. ![Image 3: Veterinary Telehealth Services](https://cdn-kdlcf.nitrocdn.com/cMcYFIddemcOYMBMSLWkeCfvQYhMPlRu/assets/images/optimized/rev-a07a029/gv-wp-landing.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/04013425/Veterinary-Telehealth-Services.jpg) As the most accessible point of contact with the practice, veterinary telehealth services deliver immense value—deepening client relationships while positioning the practice at the forefront of care innovations. 
**Veterinary Answering Service Deliver White-Glove Client Experiences**
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

 While telehealth offers veterinary expertise online, [**veterinary**](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Veterinarian) answering services provide personalized support by phone. These outsourced teams handle incoming calls, follow a clinic's protocols and workflows, interface with practice software, and ensure seamless coordination. **This immediately expands a clinic's capacity to manage client requests without adding further strain on the in-house team.** For clients, answering services deliver white-glove experiences defined by compassionate guidance and timely access to veterinary insight. Key benefits include: 
### **24/7 Support & Access**

 With extended team coverage, clients can connect with the practice anytime. This means no more unanswered calls or waiting for callbacks while a pet suffers. 
### **Expert Triage & Care**

 Many answering teams employ credentialed veterinary technicians to assess situations. This expertise ensures pets are accurately triaged and directed to proper care based on acuity of need. 
### **Appointment Convenience**

 Answering teams easily schedule appointments, coordinate prescriptions, handle billing, and complete intake paperwork to simplify demands on clients and in-clinic staff. 
### **Loyalty & Trust Building**

 By providing reliable veterinary access and personalized guidance, the veterinary answering service reinforces the practice as the go-to healthcare provider for pets. 
**How Telehealth and Answering Innovations Empower Practices?**
---------------------------------------------------------------

 With game-changing benefits for veterinary teams, it's clear why telehealth and answering solutions are skyrocketing in popularity. By leveraging technology to expand care capacity, they empower practices to deliver 5-star service while avoiding burnout. 
### **1. Staff Relief Where It's Needed Most**

 Client communication is frequently cited as the top cause of burnout by veterinary staff. Telehealth and answering teams immediately alleviate this demand, allowing staff to focus solely on patient care and relationship building. This prevents fatigue, improves job satisfaction, and leads to better client perceptions. 
### **2. Resilient Operations, Revenue Growth**

 Between surging pet ownership and staff shortages, many practices face immense strains on their operations. Virtual teams act as plug-and-play capacity to immediately adapt to periods of high demand and prevent missed opportunities. This builds resilience while increasing revenues through improved productivity. 
### **3. In-Clinic Efficiency Gains**

 By filtering unnecessary appointments, answering teams ensure only critical cases fill the schedule. This reduces delays, keeps veterinarians focused on high-acuity patients, and maximizes the use of facilities and equipment. Efficiency enables larger caseloads without demanding more resources from staff. 
### **4. Enhanced Client Satisfaction & Loyalty**

 Today's clients expect fast, always-on access paired with compassion and expertise. Meeting these demands is challenging for understaffed clinics. However, seamless telehealth and call management cultivate 5-star experiences to retain happy, loyal clients. 
**What Leading Veterinary Answering Innovations Look Like?**
------------------------------------------------------------

 To fully transform veterinary client satisfaction, veterinary answering teams must go beyond basic call handling. Leading providers blend compassionate service with veterinary expertise to act as true practice extensions focused on pet wellbeing. 
### **1. Credentialed Veterinary Technicians**

 Rather than general call center staff, industry leaders employ experienced vet techs. Their clinical education ensures expert assessment of pet health and experienced judgment-handling situations. 
### **2. Seamless Platform Integrations**

 Streamlined data sharing with practice platforms like phone and PIMS software is essential for coordination. Bidirectional integrations give answering teams the necessary context while keeping records updated. 
### **3. Protocol-Driven Care & Triage**

 Customizing clinical protocols to match each clinic's preferences drives consistency. Following these guidelines, answering teams deliver aligned, branded service even after hours. 
### **4. Proactive Support Services**

 Leading answering teams go the extra mile to book appointments, refill medications, manage intake forms, and handle billing to alleviate every possible client friction point. 
### **5. Ongoing Analytics & Optimization**

 Detailed reporting provides visibility into operational impacts and client needs. This data empowers continuous optimization and keeps management informed. ![Image 4: Ongoing Analytics & Optimization](https://cdn-kdlcf.nitrocdn.com/cMcYFIddemcOYMBMSLWkeCfvQYhMPlRu/assets/images/optimized/rev-a07a029/gv-wp-landing.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/04014035/Ongoing-Analytics-Optimization.jpg)
### **GuardianVets: Veterinary Telehealth and Answering Innovators**

 GuardianVets is an industry leader blending compassionate answering services and [**veterinary practice growth tools**](https://guardianvets.com/case-study/) with licensed veterinary expertise. Our virtual receptionist, call overflow, after-hours support, appointment management, emergency triage, and telehealth solutions help practices expand capacity and enhance pet owner experiences. Trusted by thousands of clinics, our US-based team connects directly with practice software to continue care conversations wherever they start. Support services ensure needs are proactively assessed and efficiently managed to alleviate frustrations for clients, patients, and overwhelmed teams. As true extensions of the veterinary practice, GuardianVets helps strengthen client relationships around the clock while preventing staff fatigue. Our mission is to improve patient outcomes and staff wellbeing through personalized, data-driven solutions like veterinary telehealth services. 
**Embracing Innovation: The Future of Veterinary Medicine**
-----------------------------------------------------------

 The veterinary landscape will continue evolving as pet healthcare demand grows exponentially. Leveraging telecommunications and virtual teams represents the greatest opportunities for adapting to emerging client expectations and operating pressures affecting the industry. By providing on-demand expertise and veterinary client satisfaction, these technologies return invaluable hours to hardworking veterinary teams while enabling exceptional, branded service for pet owners. Adoption will soon be a necessity as virtual care models swiftly become the norm across healthcare segments. The potential to expand care conversations, nurture relationships, and save lives makes this innovation incredibly meaningful—and one of the most exciting shifts in veterinary medicine today. Contact [**GuardianVets**](https://guardianvets.com/contact-us/) now!

enriched_points.expanded_content: Veterinary telemedicine enhances client satisfaction by providing convenient access to care, achieving treatment goals, and offering comparable satisfaction scores to in-person consultations.
Telemedicine streamlines workflows, reduces appointment congestion, and allows veterinary staff to focus on urgent cases, enhancing overall clinic efficiency and team morale.
Telemedicine expands access to veterinary care for clients in remote areas, ensuring timely support and improving the frequency of pet health consultations across diverse demographics.
